  • Integrating gen AI creative tools—The company has signed recent partnerships with startups, including Rembrand, Nova, Bunny Studio, and Spaceback, to design creative content using generative AI. This makes The Trade Desk a more holistic one-stop shop for designing and delivering ads.
  • Ad-supported is becoming more of a focus for subscription companies. Especially in a tighter economy, companies like Spotify Technology S.A. (SPOT) and Netflix, Inc. (NFLX) are working to supplement their primarily premium subscription-driven business with cheaper ad-supported plans, which increases the relevance and inventory that The Trade Desk has to work with.
  • Incredibly profitable—The Trade Desk generates a ~40% adjusted EBITDA margin, and its business is a reliable FCF generator as well.
  • Cash-rich balance sheet—Adding on top of its consistent profit generation is the fact that The Trade Desk has nearly $2 billion of cash on its books, unencumbered by debt, which gives the company plenty of flexibility to pursue growth initiatives.

Stay long here: while this stock has been painful to hold in the near term, The Trade Desk still checks off a lot of fundamental boxes. The fact that it has sustained double-digit revenue growth even amid a sliding economy and waning ad demand is a signal of strong execution. Take advantage of this slide to buy the dip.

Q2 download

Let's now go through The Trade Desk's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

The Trade Desk's revenue grew 19% y/y to $694.0 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $684.3 million (+17% y/y) by a two-point margin. Not only did The Trade Desk beat quarterly estimates, but the company's guidance of "at least $717 million" in revenue for Q3 (+14% y/y) also beat Street estimates of $716 million.

The Trade Desk is also usually generously conservative when it comes to providing guidance, with "at least" being the operative word in its outlook. For Q2, the company had originally guided to "at least $682 million" and ended up delivering $12 million (2%) over that forecast. All things equal, we should count on a similar beat in Q3.

The issue that investors are zeroing in on, of course, is deceleration: Q2 revenue slid six points from 25% y/y growth in Q1, and if we assume a similar beat in Q3, we'll see a four to five point slide to ~14-15% growth.

We do appreciate the fact, however, that The Trade Desk is keeping its head down amid macro weakness and focusing on delivering the products that can drive re-acceleration when the economy stabilizes. The company has infused its Kokai ad-buying platform with a number of AI features to both effectively target ads and measure spending. The company notes that three-quarters of ad spend on The Trade Desk is now flowing through Kokai and that customers on the platform spend ~20% more. Per CEO Jeffrey Green's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

In the aggregate, we are seeing more than a 20-point improvement across key KPIs for campaigns running in Kokai.

What's even more encouraging is that the clients who have transitioned the majority of their spend on Kokai are increasing their overall spend on The Trade Desk by more than 20% faster than those who have not. This is precisely what we believed was possible when we launched Kokai. Advertisers are getting meaningfully better returns on their ad dollars, and they are doubling down on the open Internet and on us as a result. Around 3/4 of all client spend is now running through Kokai, and we expect all of our clients to be using Kokai by the end of this year."

We note as well that The Trade Desk has restructured its go-to-market organization, following its sales stumbles earlier this year, in particular to improve cohesion between account managers, business development teams, and media traders. We're hopeful that these initiatives plus new product features, will continue to drive demand for The Trade Desk.

Meanwhile, on the profitability front, The Trade Desk's adjusted EBITDA grew 12% y/y to $270.8 million, representing a 39% margin. This is down -2 points y/y. We're hopeful that the company can announce efficiency measures to restimulate margin expansion (though at a ~40% margin level already, further expansion is tough)—though we do think that The Trade Desk's decimated valuation multiples help to make up for uncertainty.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices near $50, The Trade Desk trades at a market cap of $24.88 billion. After we net off the $1.69 billion of cash (against zero debt) on the company's latest balance sheet, The Trade Desk's resulting enterprise value is $23.19 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY26, Wall Street analysts are expecting The Trade Desk to hit $3.34 billion in revenue, or 17% y/y growth. If we place a 39% adjusted EBITDA margin against this revenue profile (flat to Q2 actual margins), adjusted EBITDA would be $1.30 billion. This positions The Trade Desk's valuation multiples at:

  • 6.9x EV/FY26 revenue.
  • 17.8x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA.

Recall that not too long ago, The Trade Desk was trading at a mid-teens multiple of revenue and a +30x multiple of adjusted EBITDA. Yes, macroeconomic tides have pushed down its fortunes slightly, but this is a company whose trajectory hasn't materially changed in the AI age (even if we access more web content through ChatGPT versus Google, we're still going to be served with ads) that has shed more than half, or +$20 billion, of market value.

Investors have more of a valuation safety net now to take a leap of faith in The Trade Desk: take advantage of this dip to buy.

data.title: PulteGroup: Weighing The Outlook's Positives And Negatives
data.description:
PulteGroup, Inc. (NYSE:PHM) has faced the effects of a weak housing market as the homebuilder’s closings have slowed and margins have declined. The housing market’s short-term outlook is mixed, balancing between consumer uncertainty and tariff pressure, but encouraging sales data and beginning mortgage rate optimism. PulteGroup has continued to generate healthy earnings regardless of the housing market’s outlook.

I initiated the stock at a Hold rating in my initial September 2024 article, titled “PulteGroup: Solid Margins In Undersupplied Industry.” The stock has since returned 6%, below the S&P 500’s return of 21%, but still slightly above the S&P Homebuilders ETF’s (XHB) stagnant return.

Weighing The Good And Bad In PulteGroup’s Outlook

The housing market has been in a slump, and PulteGroup’s recent financial results haven’t been exempted from market weakness. The latest Q2 results showed a -5.7% slowdown in closings as new home sales have remained subdued, and following a decline in housing prices, PulteGroup’s pricing power has been pressured. The gross margin declined by 290 basis points to 27.0% in the latest report. While PulteGroup maintains very healthy, industry-leading homebuilding margins, the effects of the housing market’s slowdown are increasingly seen in the company’s results.

PulteGroup is going to report its Q3 results on the 21st of October. The housing market’s outlook is very split; there are a number of positive but also negative factors. Negatives still mostly outweigh positives over the short term—PulteGroup guides Q3 closings at 7200-7600 and the gross margin at 26.0-26.5%, down by -6.6% and 255 basis points, respectively, from Q3 ’24 at the mid-point. Reflectingly, Wall Street expects revenues to decline by -3.8% to $4.31 billion and the EPS to decline by -13.5% to $2.90.

Macroeconomic uncertainty is still a major negative in the housing market outlook, continuing to control the earnings outlook. The consumer sentiment has remained weak. Trump’s tariff policies have caused cost inflation for homebuilders, latest estimated by UBS at an $8900 impact per home after the addition of the latest lumber tariffs; PulteGroup will need to pass increased costs onto homebuyers in an already cautious sales environment in order to protect margins. Most critically, mortgage rates remain quite high, with the 30-year rate at 6.34% at the time of writing. Housing affordability remains weak.

There are still clear positives in the outlook as well - the market may not remain weak forever. Firstly, the Fed chose to lower rates in mid-September, and the market is already pricing in further cuts in upcoming FOMC meetings over the upcoming year. While the Fed funds rate’s correlation with mortgage rates is not one-to-one, lower rates could start to affect long-term interest rates as well. Mortgage rates are a critical factor in the housing market’s outlook, being the most important factor affecting immediate housing affordability.

Secondly, August showed very encouraging sales data—new home sales surged by 15.4% year-on-year as prospective homebuyers have reacted to slightly lower mortgage rates. As rates have dropped some more in September, PulteGroup’s upcoming July-September Q3 report could already show a clearly above-expected performance in home closings. There is a clear pent-up demand for housing that is waiting for better buying conditions.

PulteGroup has continued to spend an immense amount on land acquisition and development—the company’s significant growth in controlled lots, shifting towards optioned lots, still positions PulteGroup for good long-term growth. The structural market tailwind from a housing shortage continues to bolster homebuilders’ capital returns and long-term growth prospects past current housing market turbulence. Once the sales environment improves, PulteGroup is poised to extend earnings.

More sustainable improvements in the sales environment remain to be seen; mortgage rates are still by no means low. Homebuilders have largely relied on sales incentives to push inventory to the market, and encouraging August sales data alone isn’t enough to establish a positive market trend. The market outlook is still clearly turbulent—finding balance amidst very mixed market indicators is challenging. Ultimately, PulteGroup still has a significantly good 23.1% return on equity in the current weak housing market environment, and the company looks to generate significant income regardless of the market outlook.

Updated Valuation

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for PulteGroup. I now expect the company to gradually return to positive sales growth through a slow housing market recovery, and that structural housing market tailwinds aid good long-term growth. I estimate the 2024-2034 revenue CAGR at 3.1% with 2.5% perpetual growth after the estimate period.

Despite a short-term profitability decline in the current weak housing market, I again estimate PulteGroup’s EBIT margin to stabilize at 20%. The cash flow outlook remains healthy, albeit PulteGroup’s growth ambition does require significant real estate asset investments.

The estimates put PulteGroup’s fair value at $159.2, 18% above the stock price at the time of writing. I believe that the current valuation provides a fair margin of safety in the investment, not yet presenting a significant buying opportunity. In my opinion, the stock would start to become attractive at an entry point near $120.

The fair value estimate is up from $149.0 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of equity of 10.31%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.10% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. Yahoo Finance now estimates PulteGroup’s beta at 1.30. With a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 10.31%.

Takeaway

The housing market’s outlook is mixed as macroeconomic factors are drifting the market in two directions. Balancing the positives and negatives, I believe that the outlook is improving, but very cautiously, as mortgage rates remain the focus point. PulteGroup will likely continue to generate healthy earnings regardless of the current housing market environment; long-term tailwinds ultimately matter more. PHM stock comes with a fair margin of safety at the current price, and I remain at a Hold rating.

data.title: Dare I Say It, Blackstone Secured Lending Fund Is The Most Overpriced Quality BDC (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I think that I will not be wrong by assuming that many of you, who have opened this article, and seeing the title of it, probably think that I do not fully understand the importance of quality, and that for long-term investors, it makes sense to pay extra for it. My next guess is that some Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (NYSE:BXSL) bulls might think that I am simply overreacting to the recent correction that has taken place not only for BXSL, but also across the entire BDC (BIZD) space.

I will try to provide as much facts and substantiated arguments to explain why, in my humble opinion, BXSL is indeed one of the most (if not the most) overvalued quality BDC stock.

Before that let me quickly disclose my previous work (articles) on BXSL.

July, 2025, before the Q2, 2025 report was circulated, I issued a relatively bullish piece on BXSL, where the conclusion was: a) BXSL's yield could be considered sustainable; b) NAV is inherently durable; c) it is a buy for income-focused investors, who are comfortable with having a flat price performance since BXSL's multiple was already quite rich.

However, while the overall thesis about BXSL's distinct quality aspect remained in tact, seeing the build-up of risks at the employment front, in mid August, I decided to write a more strategic article titled: It's All Downhill For Most BDCs, Here Is My Approach.

In the article I laid out my thinking that almost all first-lien-focused BDCs are very likely to experience a sharp correction as the lower interest rates (including the rising probabilities for additional cuts) kick in. I highlighted BXSL as a rather vulnerable example due to unfavorable exposure (high EBITDA company segment) and rich valuations. Since then BXSL's share price has plunged by ~14%.

With that being said, let's dive into my argument on why BXSL is grossly overpriced.

Why BXSL is very overpriced

I would like to start by once again emphasizing that BXSL is indeed a solid BDC with certainly above average fundamentals in almost any area (be it earnings quality, be it interest coverage of portfolio companies etc.). BXSL's historical non-accrual (consistently below 2.5%) and gradual NAV growth dynamics clearly prove that. In other words, there is no question about the validity or BXSL's premium relative to the sector average.

However, the question is about the extent of the daylight between BXSL's P/NAV and that of sector average (including closest peers).

As of now, BXSL trades at a discount of 4%, which is way below the relatively recent peak of ~25% premium. Some of you might think that this situation provides an excellent buying opportunity.

I do not think so. And here is why.

First, the sector median P/NAV is 0.83x (i.e., a discount of ~17%). The gap is just huge. Of course, as outlined above, BXSL is better than average and thus should deserve some premium. But bear with me.

Second, the only BDCs that trade above BXSL's P/NAV metric are either equity-biased or internally managed. Think of Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN), Trinity Capital Inc. (NASDAQ:TRIN), Hercules Capital, Inc. (HTGC), Fidus Investment (NASDAQ:FDUS) or Capital Southwest (NASDAQ:CSWC). All of these are positioned differently than BXSL and similar BDCs, which focus on large EBITDA ticket size first-lien lending.

In the environment like this, when the interest rates are set to go down and the competition in the space is simply mushrooming, BDCs, which do not have equity component (i.e., negatively correlated return to falling interest rates) or do not focus on lower middle market lending (where spread compression is not as pronounced) are finding themselves in a very vulnerable position. This is the case of BXSL.

Third, if we compare BXSL to more direct peers such as Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL) or Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC), we will arrive at even worse conclusion. Currently, MSDL and KBDC trade at discounts of 18% and 16%, respectively. At the same time, they carry similar capital structures (KBDC is actually much less levered), even stronger interest rate coverage levels for their portfolio companies, and better positioned borrowings profile (the right hand side of the balance sheet).

For example, speaking of leverage, BXSL has four outstanding fixed-rate notes that yield between 2.125% and 3.625% that will have to be refinanced over the next ~2 year period; most of them within 1 year. It is clear that BXSL's NII per share will not benefit from it since the prevailing cost of debt for similar fixed rate IG notes is around 5.5%.

Apart from these strategic valuation points or perspectives, here are some additional facts that make it very difficult for me to really find a justified reason to go long BXSL now.

  • BXSL's base dividend coverage is 100%. It is even lower than the sector average of 102%. In my view, BXSL will have to cut, no questions about that.
  • To strengthen a bit the previous assumption about an imminent cut, BXSL's debt to equity stands at 1.12x, which is just slightly below the usual maximum that BDCs set in order to avoid entering into a too speculative territory. From this kind of leverage base, it is hard to imagine how BXSL could drive significant portfolio growth (or a growth that would offset the forthcoming pressures).
  • If we compare Q2, 2025 NII per share result of $0.77 to that of Q2, 2024 ($0.89 per share), we will have to conclude that BXSL is an extremely interest rate sensitive vehicle. In this time period, there have been only two negative changes to BXSL's business: 1) reduced interest rates in 2024 and 2) spread compression. Both dynamics are not over, especially the former, which has clearly assumed a more pronounced trend to a further downside (i.e., cuts).

The bottom line

In a nutshell, I do not see a rational case for BXSL trading close to NAV, when directly comparable and, in some instances, even better BDCs such as MSDL and KBDC carry almost double-digit discounts.

Seeing BXSL in the BDC top valuation decline does not make sense for me either. The other BDCs that trade at or above NAV are again either equity-driven, internally managed, or carry a combination of both with a tilt to the lower middle market segment, where at least the spread compression headwind is not that strong.

In my humble opinion, BXSL has further downside to realize. The dividend cut in the near term seems almost inevitable given the direction of travel in interest rates (including the effects from the already realized cuts) and no tools in the box that could offset the pressures, such as equity monetization or balance sheet driven growth.

If, however, the BDC sector starts to recover, my expectation is that BXSL will, of course, follow, but to a much lesser extent than, say, KBDC or MSDL, which have entered a deep-value territory and thus have a more favorable base from which to jump out.

data.title: SOXX: You Cost Too Much, But You Are Still Worth Even More
data.description:
Discussing positively in the comments with some of you readers, an observation you made really made me think, which paraphrased said something like: “I know little about semiconductors, I only know that these ETFs easily manage to outperform the average annual performance of the S&P 500.” And indeed the 10y CAGR of iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), my pick in semiconductors, is about +21%, with a 3y beta vs. S&P 500 (SP500) of 1.6 and a standard deviation of 31%.

Well, going beyond any other technical consideration, we certainly cannot say that that comment is wrong; it projects a disarming truth. And assuming to be “mentally immune” to volatility, it simply makes clear that today the only thing an analyst must be able to answer in my opinion is: is this CAGR sustainable? Well, personally, on SOXX I see a “two-paths” line of analysis: EPS and optimistic prospects, prohibitive valuations. And let me say that, in my opinion, the first will prevail over the second. But let’s go in order.

SOXX

Trying to make a recap of my first coverage, a new reader should simply remember that:

  • It remains among the most appreciated ETFs, not by chance it has an AUM (assets under management) of over $13 billion and a TER of 0.35%.

  • SOXX replicates the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX), which follows the performance of the 30 largest U.S.-listed companies in the semiconductor sector.
  • The concentration in the top 10 holdings is 58% while the sector distribution is characterized by: Semiconductors 79.5%, Semiconductor Equipment 20.4%.

  • Its distinctive feature compared to its competitor VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) is that SOXX is more exposed to fabless chip designers such as Nvidia (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and Broadcom (AVGO). SMH, on the other hand, has increased allocation to memory makers such as Micron (MU) and equipment suppliers such as ASML Holding (ASML), Applied Materials (AMAT), and Lam Research (LRCX). I invite you to look into this here.

If I had to describe its 2025 in one term it would be: V-shaped recovery. After the cut in expectations post liberation day, what in my opinion allowed it to recover, as discussed here, was an incentivizing geopolitical context, but above all the maintenance (despite tariffs) of EPS growth and competitive expectations within the same tech sector.

First line of thought: What worries analysts today?

Information technology with a forward P/E of 30x already has expected valuations above 93% of the 10-year distribution, and 84% of the 30-year one. If we look at CAPE, we are effectively at pre-dot.com bubble levels. Going more into detail on the semiconductor segment, SOXX has a P/E of 33.8x, so in relative terms it also presents valuations clearly above average, and statistically the higher the relative valuations, the more the expected return of the sector contracts.

Second line of thought: “growth expectations”

I start by making a statement I am convinced of, even if it is strong:

High P/Es, close to the level of the dot-com bubble, do not automatically mean the arrival of another bubble.

The difference between today’s tech companies and those of 2000 lies in profit margins. If on the one hand they share the P/E, in 2000 it was not justified, while today it is justified first of all by high profit margins in the tech sector.

And considering that specifically, the semiconductor segment, which in my opinion is well represented on the stock market by the NYSE Semiconductor Index (SOXX’s benchmark), makes up 40% of the tech sector, this explains:

  • Expected EPS growth: +45% YoY (for the semiconductor segment);
  • Expected revenue growth: +26% YoY.

So, how can one talk about a bubble in a sector with:

  • Upward guidance: many companies in the segment have provided positive outlooks, supported by AI, data centers, and demand for HBM (high-bandwidth memory);
  • While the percentage of semiconductor companies that beat expectations is above the historical average.

If the theory behind GARP investing (which I follow closely) is correct, a sector with these characteristics works with EPS compound growth: it generates enormous cash flows and reinvests them at high ROE. It can therefore still have competitive fwd EPS, an element that would sustain relative valuations at this level.

So, in my opinion, here the P/E gives a dimension of the market price, but absolutely not of its value.

Risk

In theory, it is a cyclical sector: it alternates boom and bust (historically) every 4 years, following somewhat the logic: capacity – overproduction – correction. So, every reasoning made on EPS does not eliminate the cyclical risk – the question is, will we reach a phase of overproduction?

Simply put, we cannot rule it out, and it is therefore a risk to live with, which in the end somewhat characterizes the negative side of that 21% CAGR return (a marked standard deviation and max drawdown). What, however, could mitigate this cycle? To answer, we should look at inventories. And it is reassuring in this sense that semiconductor companies are reducing stock faster than revenue growth. This, in my opinion, is a signal that demand is solid and not “bubble-driven.”

Conclusion

I am of the opinion that SOXX’s CAGR may indeed shrink due to its relatively expensive price. But I also think it will continue to be competitive compared to the S&P 500. I rule out the bubble scenario, the sector is expensive, but it also has value: we are talking about a sector with growth expectations in terms of revenues and earnings stronger than average, that beats expectations, and grows in guidance. On the negative side, its cyclical nature, with very high volatility.

data.title: Procter & Gamble: A Premium Moat, But Valuation Leaves Limited Upside
data.description:
Company Snapshot

The Procter & Gamble Company (NYSE: PG) is one of the world’s largest consumer staples companies, with iconic brands in fabric care (Tide, Ariel), in baby and family care (Pampers, Charmin), and in grooming (Gillette). It has a presence in more than 180 countries, and 5 billion people use a P&G product every day, making it a global powerhouse in the industry.

Shares are down 8.4% year-to-date (YTD) despite rising profitability, raising the question of whether further downside lies ahead. In this article, I explore whether PG represents value in the consumer staples sector or if investors should wait for a more attractive entry point.

Strong Moat & Future Outlook

PG’s greatest advantage lies in the strength of its global brand portfolio. With household names like Pampers, Tide, Gillette, and Olay, the company enjoys significant brand loyalty and strong pricing power across its business lines. We can see the evidence of this later in the fundamentals section. These brands allow PG to navigate inflationary environments and to pass on cost increases to consumers, a luxury that should not be underestimated. Similarly, the company’s scale in procurement, distribution, and marketing further strengthens its competitive position, giving it one of the strongest moats in the industry.

While growth in mature markets such as North America remains modest, PG has meaningful opportunities in emerging markets where there is an expanding middle class and growing disposable income. Markets such as India are expanding rapidly, and PG’s global distribution infrastructure could give it the edge here. Similarly, it is focusing on its e-commerce and digital channels. In its most recent earnings announcement, CFO Andre Schulten stated that e-commerce sales rose 12% and now accounted for 19% of total sales, a channel that, if sustained, offers long-term value.

It is a capital-light business compared to some of its peers. PG is consistently generating a strong free cash flow. This underpins one of the longest dividend growth streaks in the S&P 500 (69 consecutive years of increases), which is a major draw for income-focused investors. However, while returns are robust, growth rates are lower than those of its competitors. PG must balance maintaining shareholder returns organically and pursuing new lines or M&A opportunities that could enhance long-term growth.

Despite its advantages, PG faces meaningful headwinds. Intense competition from both global peers like Unilever (UL) and Colgate-Palmolive (CL) and local, low-cost brands pressures market share, particularly in emerging markets. Also, the current tariff situation is having an adverse impact on PG and its financials. In the Q4 2025 earnings call, Schulten highlighted that $1 billion of tariff-related costs are expected in FY26. If the tariff situation were to escalate, this could cause further deterioration of PG’s profitability.

Also, consumers are increasingly paying attention to a company’s sustainability and ethical initiatives. This puts pressure on companies such as PG to undertake ESG initiatives in order to maintain and grow its consumer base. PG has stated good ambitions in this regard with a commitment to reach net zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2040 and by working to design all consumer packaging to be recyclable or reusable.

Moreover, while consumer staples businesses are resilient during economic downturns, premium products can suffer from a ‘trading down’ effect from consumers as they look to save costs. The current economic environment could drive existing consumers to cheaper alternatives or private labels, putting further pressure on PG to defend its market share.

Fundamentals: Standing Strong Against Competition

Let’s have a look at the financials of PG and where it sits in comparison to its peers.

Moreover, this net profit margin has been consistent over the last few years (it has been above 17% in every year since 2019). This consistency highlights PG’s cost discipline, which is a key advantage in a segment prone to margin pressures.

Moreover, in its most recent earnings announcement, PG stated earnings per share were up 6% versus the prior year. However, management did concede that while operating margin increased, core margin declined 70 basis points. This was largely attributed to input costs along with tariffs (Schulten highlighted that tariffs had a Q4 25 impact of $0.03 on its EPS).

While its return on capital is higher than that of Unilever’s, it is significantly below that of Colgate. This suggests that PG should be doing more to get the best return for its shareholders. However, the median rate for the sector is 7%, which makes PG a whopping 122% higher, and this suggests it’s performing admirably when compared to the industry as a whole. What’s more, it is higher than its 5-year average (14.9%), which suggests management is improving with regard to capital allocation.

The dividend yield compares well with that of its peers and has increased for 69 consecutive years. This is a key attraction for income-focused investors. Furthermore, its profitability and cash flow generating power suggest this growth can be sustained.

PG has a lower debt/capital ratio than its peers, which boosts both profitability and FCF, as less money is needed to service debt. This is valuable cash that can be invested elsewhere (such as in M&A pursuits, as suggested earlier in the thesis).

PG also has a strong overall balance sheet. It has a book value per share of $21.88 (which is significantly higher than that of its peers). This could explain its P/E ratio, which is higher than that of its peers. Given that it is not vastly higher than its peers, it could suggest that the premium placed on this company is lower than merited.

Valuation: What The Model Suggests

To aid the investment thesis, I ran a simplified discounted cash flow (DCF) model using the Gordon Growth Model. The model projects PG’s free cash flows (FCF) over the next five years, then applies a terminal growth rate to estimate long-term value. This method helps gauge what the business is worth today based on its future cash-generating ability.

Using reasonable assumptions set out below, the model yields a fair value estimate of approximately $115 per share, which is 25% below its current share price of $152 (as of 1 Oct.).

Analyst Consensus

Wall Street analysts are bullish about PG. The stock currently holds a consensus rating of “Buy,” based on the latest assessments by analysts. Of 24 analysts, 10 rated the stock as “Strong Buy,” 4 as “Buy,” and 9 as “Hold.” Meanwhile, only one analyst recommended “Sell”.

Importantly, the average price target sits at $170.18, which implies a more than 10% upside from current levels ($152 at the time of writing).

Technical Insight

As of 2 October 2025, PG is trading at $152, down 8.4% YTD. It has dropped below its 200-day moving average and is currently trending downward. It has previously bounced off a $150 support (which was also an area of resistance in early 2024), and if it breaches this level, it could trigger further downside. It may be wise to wait for a confirmed turnaround before entering. Alternatively, for investors who trade with options, it could present a cash-secured-put selling opportunity.

What Would Change My Thesis?

Based on the above, I would classify PG as a Hold. What would make me change my thesis is largely how the current macro backdrop unfolds. If the tariff situation eases, that should boost PG’s margins, and profitability will increase. That will boost EPS and provide extra funds for reinvesting. Also, if the stock pulls back further below the $150 level (perhaps to a level that would classify it as oversold on the stochastic indicator) before signaling a reversal, it will present a good entry opportunity.

Conclusion

PG is a premium-quality defensive stock with exceptional dividend reliability, but the valuation looks full. It remains a solid hold for income investors, though new buyers may want to wait for more favorable entry conditions. This could be at a level below $150, or if tariffs ease and margins recover.
